sections.title: Functions and Errors
sections.summary: Teach learners to shape behavior with functions, handle failure explicitly, and prepare for more structured project work.

sections.title: Types and Interfaces
sections.summary: Teach learners to model data with structs, attach behavior with methods, and rely on interfaces and generics to build reusable contracts.

items.title: Functions
items.summary: Introduce basic function signatures, parameters, and return values.
objectives: Define small functions with clear inputs and outputs
Read parameter and return contracts without guessing hidden behavior
items.production_relevance: Small, explicit function contracts are the foundation for readable Go code and later error-return patterns.

items.title: Closures and Recursion
items.summary: Show how functions can capture state and call themselves when the problem shape needs it.
objectives: Explain how closures retain access to surrounding variables
Use recursion deliberately without losing track of the call flow
items.production_relevance: Closures and recursion appear in handlers, callbacks, and traversal logic, but they need disciplined readability to stay maintainable.

items.title: Variadic Functions
items.summary: Introduce variadic parameters and the spread form for passing slices into function calls.
objectives: Write functions that accept a flexible number of values
Recognize when variadic APIs improve clarity and when a slice is clearer
items.production_relevance: Variadic APIs are common in formatting, logging, and helpers, but they work best when the call shape stays obvious.

items.title: Multiple Return Values
items.summary: Teach Go's multi-return style and the path toward returning explicit errors as values.
objectives: Return multiple values from a function intentionally
Explain why Go pairs useful results with explicit error returns
items.production_relevance: Multiple return values are the base contract for idiomatic Go error handling and explicit control flow.

items.title: Custom Errors
items.summary: Show how to define custom error values that expose stable failure meaning.
objectives: Implement custom error types that carry useful context
Use stable error identity instead of fragile string matching
items.production_relevance: Stable custom errors make branching, retries, and user-safe handling easier across real Go services and tools.

items.title: Error Wrapping
items.summary: Teach how to wrap low-level failures with more context while preserving inspectable error chains.
objectives: Wrap errors with contextual information using idiomatic Go patterns
Inspect wrapped errors safely with the standard library helpers
items.production_relevance: Wrapped errors let logs, callers, and retry logic keep context without destroying the underlying failure identity.

items.title: Defer
items.summary: Introduce defer as a cleanup and completion tool that keeps function exits disciplined.
objectives: Use defer for cleanup and completion tracking
Explain when deferred work improves reliability and when it obscures intent
items.production_relevance: Defer is central to cleanup in file, lock, and transaction workflows where correctness depends on predictable exit behavior.

items.title: Panic and Recover
items.summary: Show where panic belongs, where it does not, and how recover works only at controlled defer boundaries.
objectives: Explain why panic is not a replacement for ordinary error returns
Recover only at deliberate safety boundaries inside deferred code
items.production_relevance: Reliable Go code treats panic as an exceptional boundary concern rather than everyday flow control, especially in servers and workers.

items.title: Error Handling Project
items.summary: Guide learners through combining custom errors, wrapped failures, and defer-based cleanup in one focused exercise.
objectives: Build a small program that applies the section's explicit error-handling ideas together
Preserve useful failure context by wrapping low-level errors without hiding the underlying math failure
items.production_relevance: Small tools and services often need to continue handling expected failures while still surfacing enough context for debugging and review.

items.title: Functional Options Pattern
items.summary: Introduce the functional options pattern as a scalable way to configure behavior without constructor explosion.
objectives: Explain how option functions separate defaults from configuration overrides
Recognize when the pattern helps and when it overcomplicates a small API
items.production_relevance: Functional options appear in many Go libraries where configuration flexibility matters, but they should be used intentionally rather than by habit.

items.title: Structs
items.summary: Introduce structs as Go's core data-modeling tool without inheritance or hidden class behavior.
objectives: Define structs that group related data into readable shapes
Explain when passing a struct by value copies data and when a pointer avoids that cost
items.production_relevance: Struct design is the basis for every later Go model, request payload, configuration shape, and domain type.

items.title: Methods
items.summary: Show how methods attach behavior to types and why receiver choice affects semantics and performance.
objectives: Define methods on concrete types with clear receiver intent
Explain the tradeoff between value receivers and pointer receivers
items.production_relevance: Receiver choice affects mutability, method sets, and copy costs in real Go APIs, not just toy examples.

items.title: Interfaces
items.summary: Teach interface contracts, implicit satisfaction, and how Go achieves polymorphism without inheritance.
objectives: Explain implicit interface satisfaction without relying on class-style inheritance
Use interfaces to describe behavior boundaries instead of concrete implementation details
items.production_relevance: Interfaces define seams for testing, package boundaries, and flexible architecture in production Go systems.

items.title: Stringer
items.summary: Use fmt.Stringer as the first standard-library interface integration point for custom types.
objectives: Implement fmt.Stringer to give custom values readable string output
Connect concrete type methods to a standard-library interface contract
items.production_relevance: Readable String methods improve logs, debug output, and CLI feedback without scattering formatting logic everywhere.

items.title: Generics
items.summary: Introduce type parameters and constraints as a way to write reusable, type-safe helpers without reflection.
objectives: Use type parameters and constraints to generalize helper logic safely
Recognize when generics improve reuse and when a concrete function is clearer
items.production_relevance: Generics help remove duplicate collection helpers and reusable utilities while preserving compile-time type safety.

items.title: Payroll Processor Project
items.summary: Combine structs, methods, interfaces, and a small generic helper into one polymorphic payroll exercise.
objectives: Build a small payroll domain that relies on interface contracts instead of inheritance
Show that multiple concrete employee types can be processed through one common behavior boundary
items.production_relevance: Real Go services often model multiple concrete types behind shared interfaces, especially in payment, billing, and reporting flows.

items.title: Advanced Generics
items.summary: Push generics further with reusable collection operators and a clearer understanding of type inference limits.
objectives: Use generic collection helpers like map and filter without losing type clarity
Explain where type inference helps and where generic APIs become too clever
items.production_relevance: Advanced generic helpers can reduce repetition in utility layers, but they only help when the resulting API stays readable for teams.
